1: The full leadership of the post-fall remnant petitions Jeremiah — all the military commanders, Johanan son of Kareah, Jezaniah son of Hoshaiah, and "all the people from the least to the greatest" (miqāṭōn wĕʿad-gādôl). The inclusivity of the assembly (military leaders + all people) parallels the formal national assemblies of the covenant-making tradition. The decision being sought has community-wide implications.
2: tippal-nāʾ taḥinnātênû lĕpānêkā wĕhitpallēl baʿădēnû — "Please hear our plea for mercy and pray to the LORD your God for us." The phrase ʾĕlōhêkā (your God) rather than "our God" is notable — the remnant recognizes that Jeremiah has privileged access to YHWH. They identify YHWH as Jeremiah's God while asking for intercession. The MKT preserves "your God" because the pronoun reveals the community's spiritual distance.
3: The request: wĕyaggêd-lānû YHWH ʾĕlōhêkā ʾet-hadderek ʾăšer nēlek bāh — "Ask the LORD your God to tell us the way we should go and what we should do." The framing is obedience to divine guidance — they claim to want to know the way and then do it. This stated openness will be revealed as bad faith in 43:2-4, but in v.3 it sounds like genuine submission.
4: šāmaʿtî hinnĕnî mitpallēl ʾel-YHWH ʾĕlōhêkem — Jeremiah accepts the prophetic commission but establishes his role: he will report whatever YHWH says without holding anything back (lōʾ-ʾĕkhaśśēd mikkĕm dābār). The phrase "I will not withhold a word from you" is the prophet's commitment to full truthfulness even if the oracle is unwelcome. The MKT renders "I will not keep back a single word from you."
5: YHWH yihyeh bānû lĕʿēd ʾĕmet wĕneʾĕmān — "May the LORD be a true and faithful witness against us if we do not act in accordance with all that the LORD your God sends you to tell us." The oath-formula invokes YHWH as witness-judge. ʾĔmet wĕneʾĕmān (true and faithful) — the covenant-reliability doublet. The oath against themselves (ʾim-lōʾ nĕʿăśeh) is solemn and self-binding. The MKT renders the full conditional oath: "may the LORD be a true and faithful witness against us."
6: ʾim-ṭôb wĕʾim-rāʿ bĕqôl YHWH ʾĕlōhênû — "Whether it is good or bad, we will obey the voice of the LORD our God." The absolute commitment — good or bad outcome — appears to be the gold standard of submitted obedience (contrast Abraham's willingness to offer Isaac). The phrase lĕmaʿan yîṭab-lānû (so that it may go well with us) reveals the underlying motive: compliance for benefit rather than obedience as covenant relationship. The benefit-orientation will reappear in 44:17-18 (the queen of heaven argument).
7: wayhî miqṣêh ʿăśeret yāmîm — "At the end of ten days the word of the LORD came." The ten-day delay is a narrative feature: YHWH does not respond immediately. This implies genuine divine deliberation (not that YHWH needed time to decide, but that the form of the oracle required waiting). The waiting period also tests the community's patience and willingness to remain oriented toward YHWH rather than making a decision independently.
8: Jeremiah summons all the principals — military commanders, Johanan, and all the people — for the oracle delivery. The formal summoning mirrors the formal petition of vv. 1-3. The oracle is given to the whole assembly, not whispered to one leader. The MKT preserves "all the people from the least to the greatest" because the universal assembly formula matches the solemnity of what follows.
9: kōh-ʾāmar YHWH ʾĕlōhê Yiśrāʾēl ʾăšer šĕlaḥtem ʾōtî ʾēlāyw lĕhapîl tĕḥinnatkĕkem lĕpānāyw — the oracle formula grounds the divine word in the community's own petition. YHWH responds to the prayer they brought — the oracle is responsive to human seeking. The MKT renders "to whom you sent me to bring your petition" to preserve the responsive character of the oracle.
10: ʾim-šôb tēšĕbû bāʾārets hazzōʾt ûbānîtî ʾetkem wĕlōʾ ʾehĕrōs — "If you will remain in this land, I will build you up and not tear you down, and I will plant you and not uproot you"; the build/plant promise echoes Jeremiah's commissioning (1:10) and the positive side of the fig oracle (24:6). Kî niḥamtî ʾel-hārāʿâ ʾăšer ʿāśîtî lākem — "for I relent concerning the disaster I have brought on you" (niḥam, the verb of divine relenting). YHWH's relenting is contingent on Israel staying in the land.
11: ʾal-tîrĕʾû mippĕnê melek Bābel ʾăšer ʾattem yĕrēʾîm mippānāyw — "Do not be afraid of the king of Babylon, whom you now fear. Do not be afraid of him, declares the LORD, for I am with you to save you and deliver you from his power." The divine "I am with you" (kî ʾittĕkem ʾānî) formula — the Immanuel-presence promise — is the antidote to the fear of Babylon. The fear named in 41:18 as the reason for fleeing to Egypt is directly addressed and countered.
12: wĕnātattî lākem raḥămîm — "I will show you compassion (raḥămîm)"; the covenant-loyalty vocabulary — raḥămîm (womb-compassion, tender mercy) — invoked as the ground for Nebuchadnezzar's own compassion. YHWH will move the Babylonian king to have mercy on them and allow their return to the land. The MKT renders "I will show you compassion so that he will have compassion on you" to preserve the cause-effect chain: divine compassion → human compassion.
13: The negative conditional begins: wĕʾim ʾōmĕrîm ʾattem lōʾ-nēšēb bāʾārets hazzōʾt — "But if you say: We will not stay in this land." The either/or structure of the oracle mirrors the covenant's two-ways pattern: blessings for obedience, curses for disobedience. The command to stay is YHWH's word through the prophet; departure is disobedience to covenant instruction.
14: lēʾmōr lōʾ kî ʾerets Miṣrayim nēbôʾ — the specific reasons the remnant gives for preferring Egypt: no war, no trumpet alarm, no hunger. All three — war, trumpet, hunger — are the covenant curses they have already experienced. Egypt is conceived as covenant-curse-free space. The tragic irony is that Jeremiah's oracle promises those exact curses will follow them to Egypt (v.16).
15: šimĕʿû dĕbar-YHWH šĕʾērît Yĕhûdâ — "Hear the word of the LORD, O remnant of Judah"; the address šĕʾērît Yĕhûdâ (remnant of Judah) occurs throughout the post-fall chapters. The remnant is addressed as a recognized theological entity — the survivors who have the possibility of becoming the continuation of the covenant community. The oracle's weight is: what you do with this word determines whether there will be a remnant.
16: hahhereb ʾăšer ʾattem yĕrēʾîm mimmennâ šām tassîg ʾetkem — "Then the sword you fear will overtake you there in Egypt." The irony: what they flee from will find them where they flee to. Wĕhārāʿāb ʾăšer ʾattem dōʾăgîm mimmennû šām yidbaqlākem bĕMiṣrayim — "and the famine you dread will pursue you right into Egypt." The verb dābaqlākem (pursue/cling to you) conveys relentless following.
17: wĕhāyû kol-hāʾănāšîm ʾăšer-śāmû pĕnêhem lāboʾ Miṣrayim lāgûr šām — the comprehensive statement: "All who set their faces to go to Egypt to live there shall die by the sword, famine, and pestilence." Lāśûm pānîm lĕ- (to set one's face toward) is the idiom of determined direction. The MKT's "set their faces" preserves the idiom's decisiveness.
18: kăʾăšer nittāk ʾappî ûḥămātî ʾel-yōšĕbê Yĕrûšālaim — "Just as my anger and wrath were poured out on the inhabitants of Jerusalem, so my wrath will be poured out on you when you go to Egypt." The comparison to Jerusalem's fate is the most frightening possible warning: what they saw happen to Jerusalem will happen to them in Egypt. Wĕhîytem lĕʾālâ wĕlĕšammâ ûlĕqĕlālâ ûlĕḥerpâ — the fourfold shame vocabulary from 24:9 now applied to the Egypt-bound remnant.
19: dabbēr YHWH ʾălêkem šĕʾērît Yĕhûdâ lōʾ-tābōʾû Miṣrayim — "The LORD has spoken to you, O remnant of Judah: Do not go to Egypt." The simplest possible restatement of the prohibition. Yādōaʿ tēdĕʿû kî hăʿîdōtî bākem hayyôm — "Know for certain this day that I have warned you." The infinitive absolute (yādōaʿ tēdĕʿû) and the prophetic testimony formula make this the clearest possible divine warning.
20: kî hitʿîtem bĕnapsĕšôtêkem — "For you have been dishonest with yourselves"; the accusation is reflexive — the deceit is self-directed, not just outward dishonesty. Kî ʾattem šĕlaḥtem ʾōtî ʾel-YHWH ʾĕlōhêkem lēʾmōr hitpallēl baʿădēnû — the bad faith: they petitioned YHWH to find out what to do, then the oracle comes and they disobey it. The MKT's "dishonest with yourselves" renders hitʿîtem bĕnapsĕšôtêkem (you have made your souls err/sin against themselves) — the reflexive form signals self-deception.
21: The indictment stated: "I have declared it to you today, but you have not obeyed the voice of the LORD your God in anything he sent me to tell you." The final verdict before they act on their intention. The word bĕkōl ʾăšer šĕlaḥānî ʾălêkem (in all/everything he sent me to tell you) is comprehensive — not partial disobedience but total rejection of the oracle.
22: wĕʿattâ yādōaʿ tēdĕʿû kî baḥereb bārāʿāb ûbaddāber tāmûtû — "Now therefore know for certain that you will die by the sword, by famine, and by pestilence in the place where you want to go to settle." The oracle closes with the same triple judgment (sword/famine/plague) that has appeared throughout the book as the covenant-curse triad. The MKT renders yādōaʿ tēdĕʿû as "know for certain" (infinitive absolute for emphasis).
